content.Text:
[Sat 2026-04-18 23:28 PDT] Add a "Movie Autopilot" feature to this ASP.NET MVC Vidly app. This is an agentic feature where the system autonomously curates a weekly rental queue for customers based on their preferences, learning from accept/skip feedback.

Follow the existing code patterns exactly. Look at TasteEvolutionController.cs, TasteEvolutionService.cs, and TasteEvolutionModels.cs as reference for the pattern (Controller → Service → Models, with InMemory repositories).

## Files to Create

### 1. `Vidly/Models/AutopilotModels.cs`
Models:
- `AutopilotProfile` - customer preferences: favorite genres (list of Genre), mood preferences (list of string like "Exciting", "Relaxing", "Thought-provoking", "Funny", "Intense"), decade preference (string), max queue size (int, default 5), enabled (bool)
- `AutopilotPick` - a movie picked by the system: Movie reference, relevance score (double 0-1), reason (string explaining why), category (string: "Genre Match", "Emerging Taste", "Hidden Gem", "Popular Pick", "Surprise Pick"), accepted (bool?), skippedAt (DateTime?), acceptedAt (DateTime?)
- `AutopilotWeeklyQueue` - weekly queue: week start date, list of AutopilotPick, generation timestamp, total movies considered, acceptance rate from prior weeks
- `AutopilotInsight` - learning insight: type (string), message (string), confidence (double), detectedAt (DateTime)
- `AutopilotViewModel` - view model: profile, current queue, past queues (list), insights (list), customers list, selected customer id, stats (total accepted, total skipped, streak count), error message

### 2. `Vidly/Services/AutopilotService.cs`
Service with methods:
- `GetOrCreateProfile(int customerId)` → AutopilotProfile  
- `UpdateProfile(int customerId, AutopilotProfile profile)` → void
- `GenerateWeeklyQueue(int customerId)` → AutopilotWeeklyQueue — The smart part: uses rental history to weight genres, applies taste evolution momentum, mixes in "surprise" picks (10-20% from genres they haven't tried), scores and ranks all movies, picks top N
- `AcceptPick(int customerId, int movieId)` → void
- `SkipPick(int customerId, int movieId)` → void
- `GetInsights(int customerId)` → List of AutopilotInsight — Analyzes accept/skip patterns to generate insights like "You tend to skip horror on weekdays" or "Your acceptance rate is climbing — Autopilot is learning your taste!"
- `GetDashboard(int customerId)` → AutopilotViewModel

Store profiles and queues in static dictionaries (in-memory, like other services do).

The queue generation algorithm should:
1. Get customer's rental history and compute genre weights
2. Check taste evolution momentum (rising genres get boosted)
3. Score each available movie: genre match (40%), popularity (20%), recency (15%), surprise factor (15%), mood match (10%)
4. Categorize picks: top genre matches, emerging taste picks, popular picks, hidden gems (old + high rated), surprise picks
5. Select a balanced mix respecting maxQueueSize

### 3. `Vidly/Controllers/AutopilotController.cs`
```
public class AutopilotController : Controller
{
    // Constructor pattern matching TasteEvolutionController
    // GET: Autopilot - main dashboard with customer selector
    // POST: Autopilot/UpdateProfile - save preferences
    // POST: Autopilot/Generate - generate new weekly queue
    // POST: Autopilot/Accept?customerId=X&movieId=Y - accept a pick
    // POST: Autopilot/Skip?customerId=X&movieId=Y - skip a pick  
}
```

### 4. `Vidly/Views/Autopilot/Index.cshtml`
A Razor view with:
- Customer selector dropdown (same pattern as TasteEvolution)
- Profile settings panel: toggle on/off, select favorite genres (checkboxes), mood preferences (checkboxes), decade preference dropdown, queue size slider
- Current weekly queue: card grid showing each pick with movie name, genre, relevance score bar, reason text, category badge, Accept/Skip buttons
- Autopilot Insights panel: list of learning insights with confidence indicators
- Stats bar: acceptance rate, streak, total picks
- Past queues: collapsible history

Style it with Bootstrap (already in the project) and match the existing app's look. Use the _Layout.cshtml.

### 5. Update `Vidly/Views/Shared/_Layout.cshtml`
Add "Autopilot" to the navbar. Look at how other nav items are added and follow the same pattern.

## Important
- Use `namespace Vidly.Models`, `Vidly.Services`, `Vidly.Controllers`
- Use the existing repository interfaces (IMovieRepository, IRentalRepository, ICustomerRepository) from Vidly.Repositories
- Use SystemClock from Vidly.Utilities if needed
- The project uses .NET Framework (not .NET Core) — use System.Web.Mvc
- Do NOT add any NuGet packages
- Make sure all files compile — check syntax carefully

I’m adding a new MVC feature that follows the existing `TasteEvolution` pattern. I’ll inspect the reference controller, service, models, and relevant repositories/views first so the new `Autopilot` flow matches the app’s conventions exactly.
`rg` isn’t installed here, so I’m falling back to PowerShell file discovery. Next I’m reading the `TasteEvolution` stack and the repository/model types that the new feature needs to plug into.

ToolUse.name: Run Get-ChildItem -Path Vidly -Recurse -File | Where-Object { $_.Name -match 'TasteEvolution|_Layout|Repository|SystemClock|Movie|Customer|Rental|Genre' } | Select-Object -ExpandProperty FullName
